free_text: Neutron scattering differential cross sections for
23Na from 1.5 to 4.5 MeV
Orginal title in EPJ/C
Differential cross sections for neutron elastic and
inelastic scattering on 23Na
U.S. Military Academy, West Point, New York
Main reference
Preliminary data
Experiment performed at 7-MV single-
ended model CN Van de Graaff Accelerator at the
University of Kentucky with a pulsing/bunching system
located inside the high-voltage terminal.
The natural sodium sample was 13.65 g of high-purity
metal cast into a 20-mil walled polypropylene
container with inner dimensions 3.02 cm diameter and
2.27 cm height. Scattering samples were right circular
cylinders hung 7.0 cm from the center of the target
gas cell.
Time of flight method was used for determination
of incident neutron energy. Chopper and bunching
system were used to produce a 1.875 MHz pulsed beam
that was bunched to 1 ns. Prompt gamma rays were
selected using time of flight method
Gas cell was used for neutron production.
The tritium or deuterium gas is held in a stainless
steel gas cell 3cm long and 0.8 cm in diameter. An
entrance foil of 3.5 micron molybendum is used to
separate the beamline vacuum from the  cca 700torr
pressure in the cell. The cell is lined with tantalum
foil and stopper to prevent neutron production in
the stainless steel.
(ENDF/B-VII.1)
Statistical uncertainty
Preliminary data EPJ/C compiled by S.H.
S.H. Updated from the main reference NP/A
updated subentries 1,3
deleted subentry 4, same data as in subentry 5
included new subentries 6,7
Elastic scattering data shown in fig.4 of the
main reference at En=4 MeV, sent by author (J.R.V.).
Data at other energies are not shown in reference
Scattered neutrons were detected with an 11.5
cm diameter and 2.5 cm thick C6D6 liquid scintillator,
which permitted pulse shape discrimination and
rejection of gamma rays
Data table updated
Inelastic scattering data, not shown in the
references, sent by author (J.R.V.)
Neutrons were detected in an 11.5-cm diameter
x 2.5-cm thick C6D6 liquid scintillator, which
permitted pulse shape discrimination and time of
flight rejection of gamma-ray events.
S.H. Minor updates, corrected data
at 3.19 MeV
Data taken from tab. 1 of the main reference
Inelastic scattering cross sections for discrete
levels were determined from inelastic gamma ray
production cross sections as a difference of
cross section of gamma ray depopulating discrete level
and sum of gamma ray cross sections populating
this level.
Gamma-rays were detected using a Compton-
suppressed n-type HPGe detector with 51% relative
efficiency and an energy resolution of 2.1 keV FWHM
at 1.33 MeV. Detector was positioned at 125 deg.
